definition: Agricultural commodity markets are near non-linear price response thresholds when measured by major exporter stocks-to-use rather than misleading global stocks-to-use. Major exporter wheat stocks-to-use at ~16% is at the threshold where 5% production shortfall triggers 30-50% price spikes. Corn exporter stocks-to-use at ~12-14% is already below the equivalent threshold.
evidence: Global wheat at ~32.7% looks comfortable but exporter stocks-to-use at ~16% is at threshold
Chinese and Indian stocks are non-exportable strategic reserves
Non-linear price response at low stocks is one of the most empirically validated relationships in commodity markets
Pair_1 debate elevates this as 'genuinely important and underappreciated'
Vulnerability is a directly observable physical fact not a model-dependent estimate
open_questions: How quickly could exporter stocks be depleted by a multi-region weather event?
Would Chinese/Indian strategic reserves be released under extreme price pressure?
How does the protein price transmission chain (9-18 month phased inflation pulse) interact with monetary policy?
